source: Report on Malayan labour on Bangkok - Moulmein Railway__CO273:678:1
filename: Report on Malayan labour on Bangkok - Moulmein Railway__CO273:678:1.txt
summary:
- Report (dated **25 Nov 1945**) on the recruitment, transport, working conditions, disease, and mortality of **Malayan labourers** used by the **Imperial Japanese Army (I.J.A.)** on the **Bangkok–Moulmein (Siam–Burma) Railway** during **1942–1944** (with many still held into **1945**).  
- Describes the railway project: about **410 km** from **Ban Pong (near Bangkok, Siam/Thailand)** to **Tambusaya (south of Moulmein, Burma)**, built first with about **60,000 POWs** (June 1942–March 1943) and then large-scale imported “Malayan labour” (from **March–Dec 1943**, with later arrivals possibly in late **1944**).  
- Notes uncertainty in total labour numbers because Japanese claimed to have destroyed records; Japanese recollections cited **~70,000 Malayans + 8,000 Javanese** (Mar–Dec 1943) plus **~5,000** more later, while other unconfirmed claims ranged much higher (e.g., **250,000 Malayan / 100,000 Javanese** with **170,000 deaths**, explicitly unconfirmed). The author cautiously suggests **possibly ~100,000** Malayans transported, “with considerable reserve.”  
- Details recruitment methods: initially **contracts** (3–6 months, **$1–$2/day** for unskilled, bonuses, dependent allowances incl. rice, clothing/medical promises), later increasingly **coercive measures** (quotas demanded from village/Chinese leaders, transfers from other Japanese employment, estate quotas, “round-ups” including cinema audiences; reports of street “netting” in Singapore—text partly missing).  
- Describes severe conditions: overcrowded rail transport (**30+ per wagon**), long marches to jungle camps (often **1+ week**), inadequate sanitation/water, poor shelter and food, forced labour under violence; major hardship concentrated in the **central jungle/mountain section** (Keanoi valley to **Three Pagoda Pass**) during the **1943 monsoon**, with widespread **cholera, dysentery, malaria**, ulcers, and deficiency diseases (e.g., **beriberi**).  
- Key actors: report authored by **R. Boulard** (commander of **“K Force”** POW medical force from **Changi, Singapore**); references **“L Force”** (later medical force); Japanese medical/hygiene leadership including **Col. Hayakawa**; various Japanese units/“Butai” (e.g., **Kudo Butai**, **Takamoto Butai**, **Yabe Butai**) and named Japanese medical officers in incidents (e.g., **Takana**, **Hiketa**).  
- Provides mortality estimates and examples: columns to camps reportedly lost **1/8 to 1/5** en route; cholera case fatality among labourers stated **>90%** (vs ~50% in POW camps). Hospital/camp estimates compiled from interviews suggest **~23,000 deaths** in listed hospitals and **~19,000** in camps (partial coverage), leading to an overall inference of **~50,000 deaths** among Malayan labourers (about **50%**), with **most deaths in the last 8 months of 1943**.  
- Includes a catalogue of alleged **war-crimes/atrocities** (submitted to Allied HQ War Crimes Section, Bangkok, and via Form Q): forced work of sick, beatings and humiliations, abandonment of cholera cases, punitive practices, and multiple reported **killings by injections** (e.g., air/mercuric salts/morphia overdoses) and other abuses; many incident details are attributed to specific witnesses but some perpetrators’ names are unknown or given as aliases.
